narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, an LSAT score of 29, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the bottom first decile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 94.7% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to students who typically pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the first (lowest) position, well below the average of around the fifth or sixth decile, signaling to the model a significant late-stage dip in academic performance relative to peers. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also in the first position versus the typical fifth decile average, which reinforced concerns about consistently underperforming academically from the start. Your undergraduate GPA provided the only notable counterbalance among the top factors, as its value—though slightly below the average of 3.21—nudged the prediction somewhat toward passing, showing it wasn't a complete drag. Overall, these early and later academic rankings stood out as the strongest drivers toward failure in your case, outweighing other elements like your LSAT or family background when compared to typical students who rank higher in school deciles and pass the bar. While full-time status aligns with most applicants (over 90%), it didn't sway the outcome much here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average 37, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile rankings, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic male overcoming challenges. Schools value growth stories, so documenting targeted improvements could shift your profile into the passing zone for future models.
